title: Conditioning
subtitle: P(A given B): updating on what you know
realWorldHook: Every spam filter and every 'customers who bought this also bought…' runs on conditioning — updating a probability once you know something.
conceptSummary: Conditioning on B shrinks the world to B, then recounts how often A happens inside it.

steps.title: A different question
steps.body:
'What's the chance of rain?' is a different question from 'what's the chance of rain *given* the sky is grey?'

Every spam filter, every Netflix 'because you watched…', every recommendation engine runs on this move: updating a probability once you already know something.

That move is called [[conditioning|Updating a probability to account for something you now know. You ignore every case where the condition didn't hold, then recount within what's left.]].

steps.title: Shrinking the world
steps.body:
P(A | B) means: throw away every roll where B didn't happen, then ask how often A happens among what's left.

B becomes your new [[whole world|In probability this is the *sample space* — the full set of outcomes you're considering. Conditioning replaces it with just the slice where the condition holds.]]. The [[denominator|The number you divide by — the size of the world you're counting within. Conditioning shrinks it, which is why the same event's probability can jump.]] shrinks — and the probability can change a lot.

We'll track one target: **A = the sum is 10 or more** (a big total). Then we'll condition on what the first die shows.

steps.prompt: Across all 36 rolls, P(sum ≥ 10) is 6/36 — about 1 in 6. Now you learn the *first die landed on 6*. What's the chance of a sum of 10 or more now?

options.label: Still about 1 in 6 — the dice are independent

steps.revealMessage: Let's collapse the grid to just the rolls where the first die is 6, and recount.

revealByOption.a: Careful — the *dice* are independent, but the event 'sum ≥ 10' is not independent of the first die. Knowing it's a 6 really does shift the odds. Let's see.
revealByOption.b: Right instinct — knowing the first die is a 6 makes a big total much more likely. Let's confirm it on the grid.
revealByOption.c: Not zero — a 6 paired with a 4, 5, or 6 already clears 10. Let's count how often.

steps.prompt: Toggle the condition. Every roll where the first die isn't a 6 is flung away, and the survivors zoom up to fill the frame — watch P(sum ≥ 10) regrow against that smaller world.
Conditioning on 'first die = 6' pushed P(sum ≥ 10) from about 17% up to 50%. Why?

options.label: The world shrank to the 6 rolls starting with a 6, and 3 of them (sums 10, 11, 12) clear 10
Knowing the first die changes what the second die will roll

feedback.correct: Right — among the 6 rolls with a 6 first, three (6+4, 6+5, 6+6) reach 10 or more. That's 3 out of 6 = 50%, counted against the new, smaller denominator.
feedback.incorrect: Conditioning doesn't touch the second die — it changes which rolls you're allowed to count. Recount inside the slice where the first die is 6.

byOption.b: The second die is still a fair, independent roll. What changed is the set of outcomes you count over — the denominator, not the die.
byOption.c: It really does change: 6/36 (17%) overall becomes 3/6 (50%) once you know the first die is a 6. The smaller world has a much higher fraction of big totals.

steps.hint: Among just the 'first die = 6' rolls, how many reach a sum of 10 or more? Count those over 6.

steps.title: Conditioning = new denominator
steps.body:
To condition on B, make B your entire world and recount A inside it.

Don't ask 'how likely is A overall' — ask 'among the B cases, how often is A?'

This one idea is the engine behind the most counterintuitive result in all of probability. That's next.
